headline: CORN GAINS, FEEDERS DROP ON CARGILL LOCKOUT
subheadline: Grain rally meets livestock headwinds as labor dispute hits major beef plant.
lead: Corn closed $4.65, up 2 cents on short covering after Missouri Bootheel replanting reports added modest weather premium. But feeders crashed 1.2% to $361.60 as Cargill's Fort Morgan lockout of 1,700 workers signals tighter cattle movement ahead. The disconnect is telling: grains pricing weather risk while livestock prices supply chain disruption.
the_takeaway: Grains find weather floor while livestock faces processing bottleneck.
teaser: Corn finds 2-cent bounce while Cargill lockout hammers feeders.

one_number.unit: workers locked out at Cargill Fort Morgan
one_number.context: The lockout at Colorado's second-largest beef processing facility creates an immediate bottleneck for fed cattle movement. When processing capacity shrinks, feeder prices drop faster than live cattle as the backup ripples through the supply chain. The timing hits during peak spring placement season.

yesterdays_call.summary: Hormuz breakthrough removes supply shock premium, corridor still fragile.
yesterdays_call.note: Oil held gains above $100 but Iran tensions resurface with Trump's latest comments.

sections.title: Grains Find Weather Floor
sections.body: Corn gained 2 cents to $4.65 on **short covering** after Missouri Bootheel replanting reports surfaced mid-session. The bounce is thin but real, funds covered 4,000 contracts as the weather story shifts from too wet to too dry in key production areas. December corn held the gain at $4.87, up three-quarters. Soybeans eased 2 cents to $11.95 but the selling lacks conviction. **Crush margins stay firm** and the November contract's 2.5-cent decline to $11.88 keeps the inverse structure intact.
sections.catalyst: Missouri Bootheel farmer reports replanting due to dry conditions
sections.bottom_line: Weather premium returning as Belt dryness replaces wet field concerns.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: weather narrative flipped from excess moisture to drought risk

sections.title: Feeders Drop on Processing Squeeze
sections.body: Feeders crashed 1.2% to $361.60 as **Cargill's Fort Morgan lockout** hit cattle futures after the close yesterday. The 1,700-worker lockout at Colorado's second-largest beef plant creates immediate processing bottleneck fears. Live cattle held better, down just 0.4% to $244.10, but the spread between live and feeders widened to $117.50. **Placement season timing** makes this worse, spring cattle flow depends on predictable processing capacity. Class III milk spiked 1.6% to $16.92, the only livestock winner.
sections.catalyst: Cargill initiates lockout at Fort Morgan beef plant
sections.bottom_line: Processing bottleneck hits feeders harder than live cattle on timing.
sections.vs_yesterday: Feeders: yesterday's leadership completely reversed on labor news

sections.title: Energy Holds Iran Premium
sections.body: Crude gained 1.3% to $100.44 as **China raised fuel price caps** again, acknowledging the lasting impact of Iran's war with Iraq over Strait of Hormuz control, which began in March and closed the key shipping corridor. Natural gas exploded 3.8% to $3.15, the biggest single-day move in weeks. Japan's Middle East crude imports hit record lows in April, confirming the Hormuz closure is reshaping global flows. **Trump's latest Iran comments** on Truth Social kept geopolitical risk premium alive. The energy complex is pricing permanent supply disruption, not temporary crisis.
sections.bottom_line: War premium now structural, not cyclical, across energy complex.

outside_the_pit.title: NDSU study shows China tariffs cost ag $15 billion annually
outside_the_pit.body: The retaliatory tariffs are hitting harder than the original trade war, creating permanent export market losses. This structural headwind matters more for grain prices than daily weather noise.

outside_the_pit.title: Wind and solar overtake gas power generation for first time
outside_the_pit.body: April marked the first month renewables topped gas-fired plants as the energy crunch limited gas availability. The shift accelerates demand destruction in traditional energy while ag processing costs stay elevated.

outside_the_pit.title: Dust storms plague Midwest as dry conditions spread
outside_the_pit.body: Central Illinois farmer reports worst spring dust storm season in memory. The visual confirms what Missouri replanting stories suggested: the weather narrative is shifting from too wet to too dry across key production areas.

spread_to_watch.label: Feeder cattle / Live cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The ratio spiked as feeders dropped harder than live cattle on the Cargill lockout news. When processing capacity shrinks, the bottleneck hits feeders first since they still need months of feed and facilities. **The ratio is screaming supply chain stress** ahead of peak placement season.

basis.headline: Eastern Corn Belt basis firming on replanting fears
basis.body: **Missouri and Illinois elevators** report tighter farmer selling as dry conditions force some replanting decisions. Western Corn Belt basis stays soft, consistent with better planting conditions. The divergence within the Belt reflects the weather story's geographic specificity. **Soybean basis steady** as crush margins hold processors' bids firm.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: NDSU study shows China's tariff retaliation costs ag $15 billion annually, offsetting the commitment's impact. Funds covered shorts today but positioning remains defensive.

the_more_you_know.title: Why 1,700 workers move cattle prices more than weather
the_more_you_know.body: Today's Cargill lockout dropped feeders 1.2% while Missouri drought reports only lifted corn 0.4%. **Processing capacity is binary**: either the plant runs or it doesn't. Weather develops gradually over weeks, giving markets time to adjust. But when 1,700 workers get locked out of a facility processing 6,000 head daily, the bottleneck is immediate and the impact calculable. Cattle markets price infrastructure risk faster than production risk because the math is simpler.

watch_list.desc: Cargill Fort Morgan negotiation update; resolution removes feeder pressure
USDA Crop Progress; corn planting above 85% confirms Belt is on pace despite dry pockets
China fuel price cap increases; above $12/MT confirms structural energy inflation

daily_quote.text: Without a struggle, there can be no progress.

source_summary: CME, USDA, energy markets, agricultural news services
